A retained-but-altered enhancer is precisely the molecular form of “the machine is there, the setting changed.” It also matches what is known about these animals from biology entirely independent of our measurement: pythons are basal snakes that retain vestigial pelvic spurs — tiny external remnants of hind limbs — and their ZRS is reported to be weakened rather than abolished, while more derived snakes (such as vipers) carry more degraded versions. Our measurement and that biology agree: the limb change is a quantitative tuning-down of a retained enhancer, not the loss of a part.

The same logic in our own lineage: the tail

The mirror image of a limbless snake is a tailless ape. If the snake kept its limb circuit, did we keep our tail circuit?

Why this is fixed within a species: a structural argument

A reader may ask: if the setting is just a tuning knob, why is it so absolute — why are there no tailed humans, no limbed pythons? The answer is structural and follows the framework’s residual-stress logic (Section 3.4).

All forms follow the pattern: circuit kept, cis switches

The snake limb and the ape tail are two cases. The framework predicts the pattern is general: for any body feature that differs between species, the building circuit is retained and the difference is in cis. We checked eight features, each in a species that lacks or strongly modifies it, asking only whether the circuit is present (the conservation of its cis settings being the deeper, separate question).

The pattern holds across all eight: the circuit is kept, and the species difference lies in cis. This is the same conclusion as the snake and the tail, now general rather than anecdotal.

Reading cis through arrangement: the four-dimensional decomposition

Why cis can be read even when its sequence is not in hand

A natural objection arises at this point. We have said the species-distinguishing information is the cis setting, and that a cis setting is a stretch of sequence. But for most of the genome we worked from gene tables — coordinates, not letters — and an enhancer is sub-gene-level, a fragment of non-coding DNA that no gene table lists as a row. How, then, can the gene tables say anything about cis at all?

The answer is that an enhancer does not float free. It sits inside a four-dimensional arrangement — which gene hosts it, which neighbours flank it, which chromosomal block it shares, where in the locus it sits, and in what temporal order it fires — and that arrangement is readable from coordinates. We cannot read the enhancer’s letters from a gene table, but we can read the house it lives in. And because an enhancer must be physically near the gene it controls to act on it, the conservation of that house is itself strong evidence about the cis wiring. This section makes the decomposition explicit and measures it for the limb locus, where the enhancer (the ZRS) and its host (LMBR1) are both well mapped.

The four components of a cis setting that arrangement exposes

We decompose a cis setting into four arrangement-readable components, each tied to one of the framework’s existing arrangement words.

  • D1 — the host and its neighbours (one-dimensional order). Which gene physically contains the enhancer, and which genes flank it. The ZRS lives inside an intron of LMBR1, roughly one million bases from its target SHH; the host-and-neighbour identity is read directly from coordinates. (Framework word: frozen microsynteny / command bundle.)

  • D2 — the linkage block (three-dimensional contact domain). Whether the enhancer remains on the same chromosomal block — the same regulatory domain (a TAD, a topologically associating domain) — as the gene it controls. An enhancer can only act on a gene it can physically touch in the folded chromosome, so if the SHH–LMBR1 block is conserved as a unit, the cis wiring (which enhancer reaches which gene) is intact, and only the sequence value inside it can differ. (Framework word: chromosomal linkage / fault line.)

  • D3 — the anchor position (the four-dimensional coordinate). Where in the locus the regulatory genes sit, measured as the fractional position we used for the arrangement analysis. A conserved fractional position means the cis anchor occupies the same place. (Framework word: the A4 anchor.)

  • D4 — the temporal firing order (four dimensions = three plus developmental time). The colinear order of a cluster is, by the Hox logic of Part V, its temporal order of expression; the enhancer that fires first sits at one end. Reading the order reads the schedule. (Framework word: the four-dimensional body-axis map.)

The measurement: the SHH–LMBR1 house is conserved while its address moves

We measured these components for the limb locus across four species — human, mouse, chicken, and cat — whose limbs differ in proportion and whose genomes are otherwise extensively rearranged.

Method. From each species’ gene table we extracted the genes of the SHH–LMBR1 neighbourhood, recorded their order along the chromosome (D1, D4), checked whether SHH and LMBR1 sit on the same chromosome and how far apart (D2), and computed the fractional position of SHH and LMBR1 along their chromosome (D3). The fractional position is the gene’s rank position scaled to [0,1][0,1] along the chromosome, so it is comparable across genomes of very different size.

Result. The neighbourhood order is identical in all four species:

species SHH–LMBR1 neighbourhood order (D1, D4)
human EN2 << RBM33 << SHH << LMBR1 << NOM1 << MNX1
mouse EN2 << RBM33 << SHH << LMBR1 << NOM1 << MNX1
chicken EN2 << RBM33 << SHH << LMBR1 << NOM1 << MNX1
cat EN2 << RBM33 << SHH << LMBR1 << NOM1 << MNX1

while the absolute position of the locus on its chromosome varies widely, yet the SHH–LMBR1 gap stays tiny:

species SHH fractional pos. (D3) LMBR1 fractional pos. gap
human 0.980.98 0.990.99 0.0050.005
mouse 0.170.17 0.180.18 0.0050.005
chicken 0.050.05 0.060.06 0.0020.002
cat 0.990.99 0.990.99 0.0040.004

What this buys, and what it does not

This is the resolution of the objection that opened the section. The cis setting decomposes into a part we can read from coordinates — the four-dimensional house (host, linkage, anchor, order) — and a part we can read only from the letters — the enhancer’s sequence value. The house is conserved across species; the value is what differs. So the much-repeated phrase “different cis, different species” is sharpened: the difference that distinguishes species is not in the house (that is shared) but in the value inside it.

The early-development grammar: where the species is first written

Why “early” is the right place to look

Form is laid down in a temporal order. The animal-vegetal and head-tail axes are set before organs; the organs are patterned before their fine details. The framework’s residual-stress logic says the earliest decisions are the most deeply fixed, because every later structure is built on top of them. If that is right, the place where a species is most fundamentally itself — the difference that cannot be undone without making a different animal — is the cis setting of the earliest switches. So we mapped the early grammar and then measured its cis directly.

A temporal cascade of conserved switches (T1–T10)

We organize early development as a cascade of switch-stages, each a small circuit that opens the next, and ask whether each stage’s circuit is conserved across a broad span of animals: human, mouse, chicken, python, zebrafish, and the frog Xenopus.

  • T1 Pluripotency (the starting state): POU5F1 (OCT4), NANOG, SOX2.

  • T2 Organizer / axis (head-tail): NODAL, GSC, FOXA2, CHRD, NOG, OTX2, LHX1.

  • T3 Germ layers: EOMES, TBXT, SOX17, GATA4, GATA6, MIXL1.

  • T4 Anterior-posterior identity (Hox): the HOX clusters, CDX2.

  • T5 Segmentation clock: HES7, DLL1, DLL3, LFNG, MESP2, NOTCH1.

  • T6 Neural tube (dorsal-ventral): SHH, PAX6, NKX2-1, OLIG2, GLI3, FOXA2.

  • T7 Limb field: TBX5, TBX4, FGF8, FGF10, SHH, GREM1, HAND2.

  • T8 Heart: NKX2-5, GATA4, TBX5, HAND1, HAND2, MEF2C.

  • T9 Craniofacial: DLX1, DLX2, MSX1, MSX2, BARX1, EDN1, SOX9.

  • T10 Eye: PAX6, SIX3, RAX, LHX2, OTX2, SOX2.

This is itself a substantive finding: the grammar of early development — the set of switch circuits and the order in which they fire — is essentially universal across animals. By the logic of the framework, if the circuits are shared, the species differences must live in their cis settings. We now show the structural signature of that, and then measure it.

Early masters are tiny genes in vast cis landscapes

If early development is governed by cis, the early master genes should be mostly regulatory landscape — a small coding core surrounded by a large field of non-coding control DNA. We measured, for eight early master loci (each obtained as ∼300\sim\!300 kilobases of human sequence centred on the gene), the fraction of the locus that is non-coding.

This is the genomic signature one expects if the species-distinguishing information sits in cis: the protein is a shared, conserved minimum, and the control landscape — where tuning lives — is the overwhelming majority of the locus. It also answers, on our own data, the old intuition that non-coding DNA is inert filler: at the genes that decide the body plan, the non-coding landscape is not filler but the control surface.

Early enhancers are conserved in existence: SIX3, measured

The decisive question is whether the early enhancers are shared between species that look very different. We measured this for SIX3 — the earliest forebrain master — between human and mouse, two species with very different head shapes (a flat-faced, large-forebrained human; a snouted, small-forebrained mouse). We obtained ∼300\sim\!300 kilobases of human and mouse sequence around SIX3, scanned the human locus in 500500-base windows, and at each window counted exact shared 1515-mers with the mouse locus; windows with a strong excess are conserved non-coding elements — enhancers — and we excluded the small SIX3 coding body so that what remains is the regulatory landscape.

The conclusion is the same as for the ZRS, now at the top of the cascade: the cis element is conserved, and the species difference is fine tuning within it, not its presence or absence.

Measuring all eight early loci, and a proxy that fails

With mouse sequence for all eight early loci in hand, we measured each one’s human–mouse enhancer conservation the same way, to obtain a per-locus cis-strength (the summed conserved-enhancer signal, normalized to 00–11).

We then asked whether a common shortcut would have given the same answer. A frequent proxy for “regulatory landscape” is CpG density (active regulatory DNA tends to be CpG-structured), and we had computed it for the same eight loci. The two disagree sharply.

This is a methodological result worth stating plainly, because it is the kind of thing a new framework must be honest about: a plausible proxy can be wrong, and when a direct measurement is available it must overrule the proxy. We use this disagreement deliberately in the simulation (Section 8), where moving from the proxy to the measured value is part of the demonstration.

“Different early-cis means a different species” — with its scope and limits

The thread of this Part supports a strong statement: the cis state of the earliest switches is what makes a species a species. We state it carefully, because the careful version is the true one.

Interbreeding as a two-barrier problem: cis as the second barrier

Why one number never sufficed

Version 4 established that bulk genomic statistics separate clear relatives from clear non-relatives but fail in the middle: by a single arrangement distance, pairs that do interbreed and pairs that do not are not cleanly separated. Concretely, when we placed pairs on a single arrangement scale, three orderings came out “wrong”: human–chimpanzee (no viable hybrid) scored closer than sheep–goat (which can produce a rare hybrid); horse–donkey (which produce a live, if sterile, mule) scored farther than human–chimpanzee; and sheep–goat versus sheep–cow — one pair that occasionally hybridizes and one that never does — came out nearly identical on every arrangement metric we had (Jaccard 0.8520.852 versus 0.8470.847; 23/2623/26 versus 23/2623/26 one-to-one chromosomes). A single distance cannot be the whole story.

Two barriers, not one

Version 6 proposes that interbreeding compatibility is governed by two barriers, of different kinds, and that a pair must pass both.

What the two-barrier view explains

The two-barrier view resolves the middle that a single number could not.

A development simulation: structure from conserved circuits and cis settings

What the simulation is, and is not

The claims of this Part are mechanistic: form is a conserved circuit read by a cis setting, and the earliest settings are the most consequential. A mechanism should be made to run. We therefore built a simulation in which a simple embryo, given only a single starting asymmetry and a set of conserved circuits gated by cis thresholds, self-organizes a body plan; and in which changing one cis threshold — with the circuit untouched — changes the resulting form.

The model, in words

The embryo is a disk of cells (a two-dimensional cross-section). A single maternal determinant is deposited at one pole — the one symmetry-breaking input; everything else must self-organize. On this disk we run four conserved signalling systems, each exactly the kind the gene tables show is universal:

  • NODAL with its inhibitor LEFTY forms a reaction–diffusion (Turing) pair: NODAL is self-activating and short-range, LEFTY is NODAL-induced and longer-range. Seeded by the maternal determinant, this pair breaks the field into a high-NODAL region (the future organizer and mesoderm) and the rest. The condition that makes a Turing pattern — the inhibitor diffusing faster than the activator — is the framework’s G1 physics applied to two diffusing species.

  • WNT, produced at the vegetal/posterior pole and graded toward the anterior, supplies the head-to-tail (anterior-posterior) coordinate: low WNT means anterior (head), high WNT means posterior (tail).

  • The organizer (GSC/FOXA2) switches on where NODAL is high; it secretes a diffusible antagonist (CHORDIN) that locally lowers BMP, carving the dorsal-ventral axis.

  • BMP, produced broadly and degraded by the organizer’s antagonist, supplies the dorsal-ventral coordinate: low BMP is dorsal, high BMP is ventral.

The cis settings enter as thresholds: each conserved switch fires when a morphogen it reads crosses a threshold, and that threshold is the cis setting. The circuit (who activates or inhibits whom, who reads which morphogen) is fixed; the cis setting (the threshold) is the value that may differ between species. This is the simulation’s embodiment of “conserved circuit, cis setting.”

The algorithm, in full

Because reproducibility is the point, we print the core algorithm here rather than only in the bundle. It is written in plain pseudocode; the runnable Python (devo_sim.py, devo_sim_v2.py, devo_sim_v3.py) is in the bundle and follows this exactly.

# ---- GRID AND EMBRYO ----
N = 160                              # grid is N x N
embryo = disk of radius R centered in the grid   # boolean mask of cells
Laplacian(Z) = (-4*Z + up + down + left + right) # zero-flux, masked to embryo

# ---- ONE SYMMETRY-BREAKING INPUT ----
maternal = Gaussian bump at the vegetal pole, masked to embryo

# ---- FIELDS (morphogens) ----
NODAL = small random noise on embryo;  LEFTY = 0
BMP   = 0.5 on embryo;                  WNT   = 0
D_NODAL, D_LEFTY, D_BMP, D_WNT = 0.10, 0.55, 0.18, 0.16   # LEFTY > NODAL (Turing)
dt = 0.20

# ---- CIS SETTINGS = FIRING THRESHOLDS (the only species-variable values) ----
# v1: hand-set; v2: threshold = base - scale * CpG_density(gene);
# v3: threshold = base - scale * measured_human_mouse_conservation(gene)
cis.organizer_on   # NODAL level that turns the organizer (GSC/FOXA2) ON
cis.head_on        # WNT level below which head (OTX2) is allowed
cis.forebrain_on   # WNT level below which forebrain (SIX3) is allowed
cis.bmp_dorsal     # BMP level below which a cell counts as dorsal
cis.nodal_auto     # NODAL self-activation threshold

# ---- OPTIONAL LEFT-RIGHT SYMMETRY BREAKER (v2, v3) ----
lr = small bias  +b on one side, -b on the other, masked to embryo

# ---- TIME LOOP (early cleavage) ----
repeat STEPS (=1400-1600) times:
    # 1. NODAL/LEFTY reaction-diffusion, seeded by maternal (+ lr in v2/v3)
    reactN = 0.15*maternal + lr
           + Hill(NODAL, cis.nodal_auto)        # self-activation, saturating
           - LEFTY*NODAL                          # inhibition
           - 0.10*NODAL                           # decay
    reactL = NODAL - 0.12*LEFTY
    NODAL  = clip( NODAL + dt*(D_NODAL*Laplacian(NODAL) + reactN), 0, 3 ) on embryo
    LEFTY  = clip( LEFTY + dt*(D_LEFTY*Laplacian(LEFTY) + reactL), 0, 3 ) on embryo

    # 2. WNT produced at the pole, graded toward anterior
    WNT    = clip( WNT + dt*(D_WNT*Laplacian(WNT) + 0.20*maternal - 0.06*WNT), 0, 3 ) on embryo

    # 3. ORGANIZER fires where NODAL is high; it secretes a diffusible BMP antagonist
    organizer = (NODAL > cis.organizer_on)
    chordin   = diffuse(organizer) for a few steps      # spreads from the organizer
    # 4. BMP made broadly, degraded by chordin -> dorsal-ventral axis
    BMP    = clip( BMP + dt*(D_BMP*Laplacian(BMP) + 0.06 - 0.9*chordin*BMP - 0.04*BMP), 0, 3 ) on embryo

# ---- READ OUT THE BODY PLAN (cis thresholds interpret the morphogen field) ----
wn        = WNT / max(WNT on embryo)        # normalized A-P coordinate
head      = (wn < cis.head_on)
forebrain = (wn < cis.forebrain_on)         # anterior-most
trunk     = (not head) and (wn < 0.70)
tail      = (wn >= 0.70)
domains   = label each cell as forebrain / head / trunk / tail / organizer

# ---- THE CORE EXPERIMENT: same circuit, different cis ----
for setting in {low, wild-type, high}:
    change ONE threshold (e.g. cis.forebrain_on) to 'setting'; rerun; measure domain sizes
# result: the form (e.g. forebrain fraction) changes while the circuit is untouched

The only species-variable quantities are the five cis thresholds in step 4’s read-out and NODAL’s self-activation. Everything else — the update rules, the diffusion structure, the network of who activates whom — is the conserved circuit, fixed across runs.

Result 1 — structure self-organizes in early cleavage

With biologically reasonable settings, the disk organizes a body plan from the single maternal asymmetry.

Result 2 — one cis setting changes the form

The central claim of this Part is that form is set by cis on a fixed circuit. We tested it the only honest way: change one cis threshold, leave the circuit identical, and see whether the form changes.

Result 3 — driving the simulation from sequence (v2), then from measured conservation (v3)

A demonstration in which the modeller hand-picks the thresholds proves only that the machinery can work. To tie it to data, we replaced the hand-set thresholds in two steps. In version 2 of the simulation, each threshold is computed from a sequence property of that gene’s regulatory landscape (CpG density). In version 3, each threshold is computed from the measured human–mouse enhancer conservation of Section 6.5. We also added the left-right symmetry breaker so that a left-right asymmetry could form.

The move from v2 to v3 also carries the methodological lesson of Section 6.5 into the simulation: because the measured conservation disagrees with the CpG proxy (OTX2 measures strong but is CpG-poor; NODAL the reverse), v2 and v3 are driven by genuinely different numbers, and v3 — driven by direct measurement — is the one we trust. The simulation thus records, in its own structure, the rule that measurement overrules a plausible proxy.

Human versus mouse: same circuit, each species’ measured cis

The experiment the framework most wants is to run the same circuit twice — once with the human cis settings, once with the mouse — and ask whether the two body plans differ. We did it. To be fair to each species we first measured the per-locus cis-strength on each species’ own window (scanning the human locus against the mouse locus, and the mouse locus against the human locus, so neither species is privileged), then normalized each on its own scale, built two parameter sets, and ran the identical circuit (the development-simulation listing) under each.

This is the honest outcome, and we present it as such rather than manufacturing a contrast. It is not a null result; it is a confirmation. The thesis of Part VI is that the earliest cis settings are the most deeply fixed layer of the genome — the layer that changes least between species — and the paired run shows precisely that, quantitatively: at the top of the developmental cascade, human and mouse are measurably alike, and a model driven by that measurement builds alike.

Honest scope of the simulation

We restate the limits in one place, because a simulation is easy to over-read.

  • Principle, not quantity. It demonstrates sufficiency of a kind of mechanism; it does not reproduce real molecular concentrations or cell behaviours.

  • Two-dimensional cross-section. Domains appear concentric because the model is a 2D disk; the real organizing axis is head-to-tail. The concentric rings are a projection artifact, not a claim about anatomy.

  • Qualitative parameters. Diffusion constants and reaction rates are set to satisfy the Turing condition and give clean axes; their absolute values are not measured.

  • Monotone threshold mapping. “Stronger cis →\rightarrow lower firing threshold” is an assumption; the true mapping from enhancer sequence to activity is not modelled.

  • Conservation is not activity. Where thresholds come from data (v3), the datum is cross-species conservation of an enhancer, which measures its existence and rough strength, not a direct assay of how strongly it fires.

  • Two species. The measured cis values are human–mouse only; generalization to many species is future work.

None of these limits touches the two conclusions the simulation is for: that conserved reaction–diffusion circuits read by cis thresholds are sufficient to self-organize a body plan, and that changing a single cis setting changes the form. Both are demonstrated; both are exactly what the measurements in Sections 3–6.5 predicted.

Figures of the simulation

The three figures below are the actual outputs of the runs described above (the files are in the bundle). Each caption restates what is shown and the honest limit.

Version 7: making position run — form from cis, and why coordinates must be read from the genome

The simulation of Section 8.2 already shows the two conclusions it was built for: conserved reaction–diffusion circuits read by cis thresholds are sufficient to self-organize a body plan, and changing one cis setting changes the form. Version 7 extends it in two ways that turn the local/global principle of Section 12 into something you can run: it separates the form knob from the size knob explicitly, and it adds limb placement, which forces a lesson the head/trunk/tail readout could otherwise hide — that a body coordinate is not free to choose, but must be read from the genome’s own position code.

Pattern and size are independent knobs (pattern ⊥\perp size)

Algorithm (in words). Hold every cis threshold fixed and scale only the size knob (the embryo’s cell count / growth factor); read out the body-plan proportions — the fractional extent of forebrain, head, trunk, and tail along the normalized anterior–posterior coordinate. Then do the reverse: hold the size knob fixed and change a single cis threshold.

Limb placement forces the coordinate-reading lesson (a tetrapod extension)

The readout so far labels forebrain/head/trunk/tail from the anterior–posterior coordinate. A tetrapod also carries paired limbs at specific anterior–posterior positions, and how we obtain those positions is the whole point.

The wrong way, and what it produces.

If the limb anterior–posterior coordinates are hand-set — numbers we simply choose — the limb fields land at arbitrary places and the body is malformed: a forelimb and a hindlimb at positions with no relation to the axial plan. A deformity is the honest result of inventing a coordinate.

The right way: read the coordinate from the Hox arrangement.

The Hox cluster is colinear — genomic order tracks the head-to-tail axis (Section 1.1, and the fourth, temporal component of Section 5). So the limb coordinate is already written, as a position in the cluster. Reading the HOXA gene order, the forelimb field maps to the Hox6 level (normalized anterior–posterior ≈0.52\approx 0.52) and the hindlimb to Hox10 (≈0.77\approx 0.77); placing each limb field where the embryo’s anterior–posterior readout equals its Hox-derived coordinate yields a correctly proportioned tetrapod.

# ---- READ THE LOCAL COORDINATE FROM THE GENOME (Hox colinearity) ----
hox_order = gene_table.cluster_order('HOXA')       # 1D genomic order = A-P order
frac      = {g: rank(g, hox_order)/(len(hox_order)-1) for g in hox_order}
ap_fore   = frac[paralog_group(6)]     # forelimb  ~ Hox6   -> ~0.52
ap_hind   = frac[paralog_group(10)]    # hindlimb  ~ Hox10  -> ~0.77

# ---- PLACE LIMB FIELDS WHERE THE EMBRYO A-P READOUT MATCHES ----
wn = WNT / max(WNT on embryo)                       # normalized A-P coordinate (as before)
forelimb = limb_field_at(wn ~= ap_fore)             # not hand-set: read from frac{}
hindlimb = limb_field_at(wn ~= ap_hind)
# Hand-setting ap_fore/ap_hind to arbitrary numbers instead -> malformed body.
The lesson, and its honest boundary.

A body coordinate is a local coordinate (Section 3.5): it must be read from the genome’s position code — here the Hox arrangement — not invented. This is the constructive, runnable form of “position is encoded, and read.” Its boundary is exactly the local/global split: the genome cleanly gives order and identity (forelimb anterior to hindlimb; which paralog marks which limb), and that is what the simulation reads correctly; the absolute metric placement — how large, how far in real units — belongs to the growth/timing layer, i.e. to the global size parameter (Sections 12, 11), which this simulation does not set. So even in limb placement, form (order/identity) is local and read from sequence, while size (absolute extent) is global and is not. The honest limits of Section 8.8 carry over unchanged, with one addition: species-specific limb specializations (a bat’s wing proportions, an elephant’s trunk) are the variable layer, out of scope without species-specific regulatory data — like size itself.

Where the size difference is stored: runtime and growth cis

A puzzle the paired run raised

The human-versus-mouse paired run (Section 8.7) produced nearly identical body plans because the measured early cis of the two species is nearly identical. But a human and a mouse differ enormously in size. If the difference is not in the early cis, where is it? The answer follows from a simple, correct observation: early embryos are similar in size; the size difference between a human and a mouse appears later, during gestation and growth. So the difference cannot be stored in the early organizer cis we measured. It must be stored in two other places — and our framework names both.

Two stores, neither of them early cis

  • Store 1 — the runtime (duration). How long an animal gestates and grows is a Layer-2 quantity: human gestation is about 280280 days, mouse about 2020; time to maturity differs likewise. The genome supplies the growth machinery; the runtime sets the duration for which it runs. This is exactly the two-layer asymmetry — the genome encodes the machine, the cell sets “how much / how long.” Honestly marked: gestation and growth period are off-genome quantities, so this store is literature, not something we measure from sequence.

  • Store 2 — the growth cis (late in the cascade). The growth circuit — IGF1, IGF2, HMGA2, the growth-hormone axis — acts during the growth phase, not at gastrulation. Its enhancers are cis, written in the sequence, but they sit late in the developmental cascade. By the depth law of Section 3.5, a late cis element is less deeply fixed than an early one, and so should be more divergent between species. If that is true, the growth cis is exactly where the size difference is stored — and unlike the runtime, this store we can measure.

The prediction, and its direct test

The depth law makes a sharp, falsifiable prediction. We measured the early organizer cis and found it strongly conserved between human and mouse. If the depth law is right, the growth cis (IGF1, IGF2, HMGA2) should be measurably less conserved between the same two species. We obtained the human and mouse sequence for the three growth loci (∼320\sim\!320– 440440 kilobases each) and measured their human–mouse enhancer conservation by exactly the method used for the early loci (conserved-enhancer signal = summed peak shared-1515-mer windows; and, as an independent check, the overall shared-1515-mer fraction of the locus).

What this establishes

This is a clean confirmation of the user-anticipated picture and of the depth law at once. The reasoning chain closes:

  • Early embryos are similar in size, so the size difference is not set early.

  • The early organizer cis is strongly conserved between human and mouse (measured), so it does not store the difference — consistent with the paired run building nearly identical plans.

  • The growth cis (IGF1/IGF2/HMGA2) is 8.3×8.3\times less conserved human–mouse (measured), so it is the least-constrained layer — consistent with the depth law’s prediction that the late layer diverges most. We are careful about what this does and does not show: “least conserved” is not the same as “stores the value.” A direct test on the 48×48\times human–elephant pair (Section 11.5) finds the non-imprinted growth cis (IGF1, HMGA2) at the unrelated-sequence noise floor, so the size magnitude is not recoverable as a conserved cis element there. The honest reading is that the growth cis is the most divergent layer, not a sequence store of the magnitude.

  • The remaining part of the difference — how long the growth circuit runs — is the runtime (gestation and growth duration), a Layer-2 quantity off the genome.

A direct 48×48\times test, by interpretation: the IGF1 house versus the unreadable value

We ran the framework’s own interpretation pipeline (Section 6: stiffness signal →\to shells →\to anchors), not kk-mer counting, on the IGF1 locus of the African elephant (∼48×\sim\!48\times a human in mass) and of the human, in matched gene±150\pm150 kb windows (igf1_4d_interpret.py; derived/igf1_4d_interpret.json).

This result also strengthens, with a second independent measurement, the depth law registered as Hypothesis H-cis3: the earlier a cis element acts, the more deeply it is fixed. We now have it both ways — the early loci measure as highly conserved, and the growth loci, acting later, measure as markedly less conserved, by the same method on the same species pair.

Where body size lives: an open question, stated honestly

We pursued the body-size question further than any single locus, and the honest summary is that we found no decisive single store. Several factors were examined and each contributes a little; none dominates. (i) Per-locus cis: the 4D interpretation of growth and skeletal receptor loci (IGF1, FGFR3, NPR2, SOX9, RUNX2, IHH, PTHLH) finds the house (shell/anchor architecture) largely shared between species of vastly different size; the magnitude is not localized there. (ii) Cell size is conserved across mammals (mouse to elephant), so body mass is carried by cell number, not cell size. (iii) Cell number is the product of division rate and growth duration; in the mouse–human comparison this maps onto species-specific developmental tempo (a runtime/biochemical quantity, e.g. protein-stability differences), not onto a sequence store — for the elephant, only the duration (gestation) is directly measured, the tempo inferred. (iv) Within a species, human-height GWAS distributes the trait across hundreds of loci, each contributing slightly, jointly explaining only a fraction of heritability; the remainder is non-shared/regulatory. (v) A test for height-variant enrichment in 4D stiffness anchors/soft shells at IGF1 was negative — functional regulatory position (open chromatin, TF sites) is not predicted by stiffness geometry, marking a real limit of the method.

We also checked the global genome, not only single windows: across whole genomes from choanoflagellates and colonial algae to early animals, the global soft-shell fraction and its clustering did not differ with multicellular grade. So size is not visibly stored globally either, by this measure.

Open items (Version 7).

Three concrete next steps are recorded so others can extend this (the method is meant to be added to, not closed). (i) A functional instrument for the value. Reading the size value — not just where it is and is not — needs expression, hormone-output, or methylation data, or many-individual (GWAS-scale) association; single-locus sequence comparison is now exhausted. (ii) Complete the felid let-7 control. Tiger HMGA2 is unannotated in the current assembly (only HMGA1 is placed); a synteny-guided tiger HMGA2 3′3'UTR region would close the within-family let-7 test of Section 11.8. (iii) Read more axes of position. The form work reads the anterior–posterior (Hox) axis; reading the proximal–distal (ZRS/SHH) and dorsoventral (Dlx/Tbx/Wnt) coordinates from the genome would extend Section 10 beyond one cluster — “read the whole position code, not a single map.” All inputs to be recorded as public accessions/ranges in DATA_LOCK (never raw genomes; the 250 MB ceiling holds).

Version 7: a lineage-controlled test — the regulatory-density “size signal” is lineage, not size

The open question above is honest but improvable. Version 6 marked that “a global, multi-locus analysis we did not perform could revise it,” and it stated the governing discipline for any cross-species size claim: compare only within a family, never as a gradient of descent. Version 7 executes exactly that discipline with two new tests. Both sharpen the distributed-size conclusion by removing a tempting local store; neither overturns it.

Test A — dosage across ∼8000×\sim\!8000\times body mass.

We counted the copy number of the growth/hormone/appetite circuit (the accelerators and brakes of Section 2, plus the central appetite controllers MC4R, MC3R, LEPR, POMC, NPY, CARTPT, PCSK1, BDNF, SIM1, and the adapters IRS1/2/4) in four annotated genomes ordered by body mass — mouse (0.0250.025 kg), cat (44 kg), human (6262 kg), tiger (200200 kg). Algorithm (in words). For each genome’s GFF, count gene features per symbol; for a gene family, count members by name prefix (GH∖\backslashd/CSH, PRL, IGFBP, SSTR); align the count vector to body mass; a feature “tracks size” only if its count is monotone in mass and the change exceeds one copy.

MASS = {mouse:0.025, cat:4, human:62, tiger:200}          # kg
SINGLE   = [GHR, IGF1, IGF2, IGF1R, IGF2R, INSR, PRLR, SST,
            MC4R, MC3R, LEPR, POMC, NPY, CARTPT, PCSK1, BDNF, SIM1, IRS1, IRS2, IRS4]
FAMILIES = {PRL: '^PRL', GH_CSH: '^(GH#|CSH)', IGFBP: '^IGFBP#', SSTR: '^SSTR#'}

def counts(gff):                          # one pass over the GFF
    for line in gff where feature == 'gene':
        name = attribute 'Name' or 'gene'
        if name in SINGLE:           single[name] += 1
        for fam, pattern in FAMILIES:
            if name matches pattern: fam_set[fam].add(name)
    return single, {fam: size(fam_set[fam])}

for gene in SINGLE:                        # species ordered small -> big mass
    row   = [count[sp][gene] for sp in sort_by(MASS)]
    label = 'SIZE-TRACKING' if (row is ascending and row[-1]-row[0] > 1) else 'flat'
# RESULT: every SINGLE gene -> flat (1:1:1:1) across ~8000x mass. Only PRL (~29 in mouse)
#         and GH/CSH (5 in human) vary -> a LINEAGE (placental) expansion, not a size signal.

(An intra-development correction is kept visible: an equal-length Hamming comparison initially made GDF15 and IRS4 look divergent; a proper global alignment shows them conserved (96.4%96.4\%, 97.1%97.1\%). The artifact was the equal-length method, not the biology.)

Test B — the decisive within-family control.

We measured regulatory-element density — CpG-island count per 100100 kb, the same kind of A4 feature used in the telomerase test of Section 3.4 — at the five growth-plate skeletal-effector loci (IHH, NPR2, FGFR3, PTHLH, SOX9), the genes where bone length, and therefore body size, is physically set. Algorithm (in words). Slide a 11 kb window by 500500 bp; call a window a CpG island when its GC fraction exceeds 0.500.50 and its observed/expected CpG ratio exceeds 0.600.60; report islands per 100100 kb for each locus; take the big/small ratio per locus; require replication in an independent family before calling a size signal.

def cpg_islands_per_100kb(seq, win=1000, step=500):        # O(n) via prefix sums
    cC, cG, cCG, cGC = cumsum(C), cumsum(G), cumsum(CpG), cumsum(G or C)
    islands = 0
    for s in range(0, len(seq)-win+1, step):
        e   = s + win
        gc  = (cGC[e]-cGC[s]) / win
        c,g = cC[e]-cC[s], cG[e]-cG[s]
        oe  = (cCG[e]-cCG[s]) * win / max(1, c*g)          # observed/expected CpG
        if gc > 0.50 and oe > 0.60: islands += 1
    return islands / (len(seq)/1e5)

GROWTH_PLATE = [IHH, NPR2, FGFR3, PTHLH, SOX9]             # bone-length effectors
for gene in GROWTH_PLATE:
    small, big = order_by_mass(species_for(gene))
    signal     = density[big] > density[small]             # does the big animal have more islands?
# CROSS-LINEAGE (mouse 0.025kg vs elephant 3t):  big>small at 5/5   (looks like a size code)
# WITHIN-FAMILY  (cat 4kg     vs tiger 200kg):    big>small at 1/5   (ratios ~0.83,0.63,1,1,0.95)
# -> the 5/5 was LINEAGE, not size. Admit a size signal only if it REPLICATES within a family.

This is the lineage-controlled test Version 6 called for and did not have. Its result is a clean negative: it removes a tempting local store and leaves the distributed/global reading standing.

Version 7: a stop-coordinate test — the let-7/HMGA2 growth brake is conserved

A different and sharper challenge reframes size not as a stored magnitude but as a stop coordinate: perhaps growth simply runs until a genomically written brake fires, and the brake’s position is the size. The strongest molecular candidate is the let-7/HMGA2 axis: HMGA2 is the single strongest body-size/height locus known, and the microRNA let-7 represses HMGA2 to terminate growth. The number of let-7 target sites in the HMGA2 3′3'UTR is the brake’s strength — a specific sequence coordinate one can count, not a distributed quantity. Algorithm (in words). A let-7 family site is the 77-mer seed match CTACCTC (and the 11A variant TACCTCA) on the mRNA sense strand (its reverse complement on the antisense strand for a genomic region); count the matches in the densest 44 kb window of the 3′3'UTR. The method was validated on the human HMGA2 3′3'UTR, which returns the textbook ∼8\sim\!8 sites.

SEEDS = ['CTACCTC', 'TACCTCA']          # let-7 7-mer seed + 1A variant (mRNA sense strand)

def let7_sites(utr, genomic=False, win=4000):
    patterns = SEEDS + (reverse_complement(SEEDS) if genomic else [])
    hits = [i for i in positions(utr) if utr[i:i+7] in patterns]
    return max over h in hits of count(x in hits : h <= x < h+win)    # densest 4 kb window

# validated on the human HMGA2 3'UTR -> ~8 (textbook).
# RESULT: human 8, elephant 8, cat 8 ; mouse 10 (rodent lineage).
# the brake COUNT is conserved across 1000x mass -> the size difference is in WHEN it fires
# (timing, a runtime quantity), not in the number of sites.
# (cat predicted model was 3'UTR-truncated and excluded; count recovered from the genomic 3'UTR.)

Like Test B, this is a lineage-controlled negative: the “grow until a coordinate” reframing is attractive, but the coordinate it points to is conserved, so it does not store the magnitude either. Together, Sections 11.7–11.8 convert Version 6’s open question into a strengthened one: two further tempting local stores (regulatory density; the developmental brake) are removed under proper controls, and the value continues to behave as a global, multi-locus parameter — in the genome, but not localizable — whose actual setting is read only from the functional/runtime layer (Section 12).

Version 7: the 4D plan — form is a local arrangement, size is a global parameter

Three results in this paper have, until now, stood beside one another without a single statement that binds them: (a) the order of the letters is a local three-dimensional coordinate on the helix (Section 3.5); (b) a cis setting is read through a four-dimensional arrangement whose fourth axis is developmental time (Section 5), of which the colinear Hox cluster is the body-scale instance; and (c) the size value is not at any one place — it behaves as a sum over loci and over runtime (Section 11), a conclusion the Version-7 lineage-controlled tests (Sections 11.7–11.8) strengthen by removing two tempting local stores. Version 7 states the principle that unifies them.

The principle.

The genome’s design is four-dimensional in the plainest sense: it specifies a local three-dimensional arrangement at each developmental stage, and the stages are unrolled in time. From this one statement follows the asymmetry that has organized the whole paper, now in its sharpest form:

  • Form/position is local. It is read off the arrangement at a place — which shell, which anchor, which fractional coordinate, which firing order. You can point to it. The helix coordinate, the A4 anchors, and the Hox map are all instances, and the form-from-cis simulation of Section 10 reads exactly these local coordinates to build a body.

  • Size/quantity is global. It is a parameter of the whole trajectory — rate ×\times duration, summed over thousands of small per-locus settings. It is not localizable to any single element. This is precisely why every local probe in the size investigation came back conserved: dosage across 8000×8000\times mass, regulatory density under a within-family control, and the let-7 stop-coordinate are all flat or lineage-driven, because size is not a local thing to find. To read it one must read the whole — the runtime, or many loci jointly.

Scale-invariance.

The same local/global split holds at every scale, which is why the framework’s molecular and organismal claims rhyme. At the molecule, a condensate’s interface position is local (set by the sequence, γ\gamma) while its fill is a global, runtime quantity (ϕ\phi, Section 4.1); at the organism, a limb’s position is local (read from the Hox arrangement) while body size is global. The principle does not change with scale; only the parts it names do.

Distinction from the four-dimensional decomposition.

Section 5 decomposes a single cis setting into four arrangement-readable components (host, linkage block, anchor position, firing order) — it is the reading method for a local element. The present principle is the higher-level statement of which that decomposition is one application: the decomposition reads a local coordinate; the principle says which traits are local at all, and which are global and therefore unreadable from any single coordinate.

Version 7: making the switch run — the condensate and response switches

Section 4.1 gave the two knobs (γ\gamma, ϕ\phi) and the capacity identity Nveff≈ϕVcoreN\,v_{\mathrm{eff}}\approx\phi\,V_{\mathrm{core}}, but left the switch — what happens as a domain crosses capacity — as a statement rather than a runnable object. Version 7 makes it a deterministic, reproducible model, because the switch is the mechanism that explains how a small, distributed (global) change in setting can produce a large, discrete outcome — the sharpness behind both body-size differences and obesity.

Model 1 — the condensate switch.

Read the firm interface as a two-state order parameter ss in a double-well free energy F(s;h)=14s4−12γs2−hs,h=h(ϕ)=k(ϕ−ϕc),F(s;h)\;=\;\tfrac14 s^4 \;-\; \tfrac12\,\gamma\,s^2 \;-\; h\,s, \qquad h=h(\phi)=k\,(\phi-\phi_c), where γ\gamma (sequence-set, Layer 1) fixes the barrier — the threshold — and ϕ\phi (runtime, Layer 2) tilts the wells. Algorithm (in words). Relax ss by gradient descent ṡ=−∂F/∂s=−(s3−γs−h)\dot s=-\partial F/\partial s=-(s^3-\gamma s-h) to the nearest stable state; sweep ϕ\phi upward and then downward, each time starting from the previous state, so the tracked state reveals both bistability and hysteresis. Result. The state flips almost vertically as ϕ\phi crosses the γ\gamma-set threshold (maximum sensitivity ds/dϕ≈438\mathrm{d}s/\mathrm{d}\phi\approx 438 in the locked run) and the up- and down-sweeps differ (a hysteresis loop): two cells — or two animals — with the same γ\gamma and only a small difference in ϕ\phi can occupy opposite states (lean/obese, small/large). Code: stiffness_switch.py.

# F(s; h) = s^4/4 - gamma*s^2/2 - h*s        (h = tilt set by fill phi)
dF(s, h, gamma) = s**3 - gamma*s - h
h(phi)          = k * (phi - phi_c)           # fill phi -> tilt of the two wells

def track(phis, gamma, s_start):              # gradient descent, CARRY state -> hysteresis
    s = s_start
    for phi in phis:
        repeat 2000x: s -= 0.05 * dF(s, h(phi), gamma)    # relax to nearest stable well
        record s
    return states

up   = track(phis_increasing, gamma, s_start=-1.2)
down = track(phis_decreasing, gamma, s_start=+1.2)
# RESULT: state flips almost vertically as phi crosses the gamma-set threshold
#         (max ds/dphi ~ 438); up-sweep != down-sweep (hysteresis).
#         SAME gamma (conserved machine) + a small d.phi near the boundary -> OPPOSITE states.
Model 2 — the response switch (the cell that receives the hormone).

The concrete biology behind the abstract switch is the cell-cycle restriction point: the Rb–E2F system is a known bistable, hysteretic switch. The input is the hormone/mitogen level the cell receives (the response-side ϕ\phi); the conserved switch hardware (the γ\gamma side) reads it and commits to divide sharply. Algorithm (in words). Integrate a positive-feedback rate law Ė=b+k1S/(Km+S)+gEn/(Kdn+En)−dE\dot E = b + k_1\,S/(K_m+S) + g\,E^{n}/(K_d^{n}+E^{n}) - d\,E to its stable fixed point; sweep the input SS up and down for hysteresis; vary the feedback gain gg (the response sensitivity, a setting) and watch the commit threshold move. Result. A more sensitive cell commits to divide at a lower hormone level, so it divides more and the tissue grows larger — the response setting (not the hardware) sets the size outcome. Code: response_switch.py.

def steady_E2F(S, gain, n=4, Kd=1, d=1, b=0.02, k1=1, Km=1):
    # dE/dt = b + k1*S/(Km+S) + gain*E^n/(Kd^n + E^n) - d*E   (positive feedback -> bistable)
    E = 0
    repeat 4000x: E = max(0, E + 0.02*(b + k1*S/(Km+S) + gain*E**n/(Kd**n + E**n) - d*E))
    return E

up   = sweep(S_increasing, gain=1.6, start=0)        # sharp COMMIT-to-divide
down = sweep(S_decreasing, gain=1.6, start=high)     # hysteresis (bistable)
for gain in [low, mid, high]:
    curve = [steady_E2F(S, gain) for S in S_range]
# RESULT: higher sensitivity (gain) -> commits to divide at a LOWER hormone level
#         -> more divisions -> bigger tissue. The SETTING, not the hardware, sets the size outcome.

Version 7: how the two knobs reconnect — a target →\to rate →\to stiffness hypothesis

The local/global rule (R18) and the switch (R19) leave one honest loose end: if the size value is a global parameter set over the whole run, how is it physically imposed on a tissue? We record a candidate mechanism as an explicit hypothesis, kept separate from the verified results.

Two worked examples, step by step

Part II gave a worked example — the neighbourhood of the E. coli replication origin — so that a reader could follow the framework’s pipeline by hand. In the same spirit, and because this Part rests on measurements a reader will rightly want to reproduce, we walk through the two central cis measurements end to end. Each is written so that someone new to the framework can repeat it from the public assemblies with the scripts in the bundle.

Worked example 1: reading the snake’s limb enhancer (ZRS)

The question.

Did the limbless python delete its limb enhancer, or merely turn it down? This is a question about a cis setting, and it is answerable by comparing sequences.

Step 1 — locate the locus from coordinates we already have.

From the human gene table, SHH sits on chromosome 7 at 155,799,980155{,}799{,}980–155,812,463155{,}812{,}463, and LMBR1 — the gene whose intron hosts the ZRS — at 156,669,012156{,}669{,}012–156,893,183156{,}893{,}183. The ZRS is therefore expected near the SHH-distal end of LMBR1, about one million bases from SHH. We request the whole span as one region: NC_000007.14:155799980–156893183.

Step 2 — obtain the sequence as a region, not a genome.

Through the NCBI Genome Data Viewer we download the FASTA for exactly that range. It is 1,093,2041{,}093{,}204 bases — about one megabyte of text, not the multi-gigabyte whole chromosome — which is why the reproducibility bundle stays small. We do the same for the python, whose assembly is scaffold-level: LMBR1 sits on scaffold NW_006534369.1, so we take NW_006534369.1:1–150000.

Step 3 — find the ZRS window in the human locus.

The region begins at genomic coordinate 155,799,980155{,}799{,}980, so a feature at genomic position pp sits at offset p−155,799,980p-155{,}799{,}980 in our file. The ZRS lies at roughly 156,790,500156{,}790{,}500–156,791,800156{,}791{,}800, i.e. offset ≈990,520\approx\!990{,}520–991,820991{,}820. We cut that 1,3001{,}300-base window as the human ZRS.

Step 4 — compare by exact shared words, with controls.

We ask how many exact 1515-base words (1515-mers) the human ZRS window shares with the entire python LMBR1 region. A 1515-mer is long enough that an exact match almost never happens by chance — in 150150 kilobases the expected number of matches to a given 1515-mer is about 10−910^{-9} — so a large count of shared 1515-mers is direct evidence of conserved sequence, with no alignment heuristics or scoring choices to argue about. To keep ourselves honest we run the same count on three controls: the LMBR1 promoter, an arbitrary intronic window, and a SHH coding exon.

Step 5 — read the result.

The ZRS window shares 112112 exact 1515-mers with the python; the LMBR1 promoter shares 77; the random intron shares 11; the SHH exon shares 00. The ZRS signal stands 112112-fold above the random-intron control. The snake keeps a recognizable ZRS. The tidy “the enhancer was deleted” story is false for this animal.

Step 6 — look inside the enhancer.

Mapping which bases of the human ZRS are covered by shared 1515-mers, 18%18\% of the 1,3001{,}300-base window is conserved — a conservative floor, since an exact 1515-mer is broken by even one substitution (a local alignment of the same windows scores about 77%77\% identity). The conserved material clusters in a core (longest conserved blocks ∼58\sim\!58, ∼38\sim\!38, ∼29\sim\!29 bases); the long diverged stretches lie in the flanks. The reading is conserved core, diverged flanks — a retained enhancer that has been tuned, not a deleted one.

What we may conclude, and what we may not.

We may conclude that the python retains a conserved ZRS core, which refutes deletion and is consistent with the vestigial pelvic spurs pythons still grow. We may not conclude, from sequence alone, exactly how much the enhancer’s activity is reduced; conservation constrains activity but is not a direct assay of it. That is the honest edge of the measurement, and we mark it.

Worked example 2: measuring an early-development cis setting (SIX3)

The question.

The earliest forebrain master is SIX3. Human and mouse have very different head shapes. Is that difference carried by the presence or absence of SIX3’s enhancers, or by fine tuning within enhancers that both species share?

Step 1 — obtain matched regions in two species.

From the gene tables we find SIX3 and take ∼300\sim\!300 kilobases around it in each species, enough to include the distal enhancers that sit far from the gene: human NC_000002.12:44791702–45096071 and mouse NC_000083.7:85771036–86083619. Each is about a third of a megabyte of text.

Step 2 — scan the human locus in windows.

We slide a 500500-base window along the human locus in 100100-base steps. For each window we count the exact 1515-mers it shares with the entire mouse locus. A window that shares many 1515-mers is a stretch of sequence conserved between the two species — a conserved non-coding element, i.e. an enhancer (or the gene itself).

Step 3 — separate the gene from its enhancers.

The SIX3 coding body occupies only about 44 kilobases (offset ≈150,000\approx\!150{,}000–154,369154{,}369 in our window). We exclude it, so that what remains is the regulatory landscape — the conserved non-coding blocks, which are the enhancers.

Step 4 — read the conserved enhancers.

Twenty-eight conserved non-coding enhancers are shared between human and mouse, with a total conservation signal of 4,3414{,}341 (the summed peak shared 1515-mer counts). The two strongest sit far upstream — one about 102102 kilobases away (peak 402402), another about 139139 kilobases away (peak 346346) — exactly where SIX3’s known distal forebrain enhancers are reported to lie, a reassuring external check that the method finds real enhancers and not artefacts.

Step 5 — the conclusion, and the scope.

Between two species of markedly different head and brain proportion, the forebrain enhancers are conserved in existence. So the species-distinguishing difference is not the gain or loss of these enhancers; it is fine tuning within them — the same reading the ZRS forced for the limb, now at the top of the cascade. The scope is honest: we measured conservation (the existence and rough strength of each enhancer) between two species, not the precise activity difference between the human and mouse versions, and not a panel of many species.

Why this matters for the whole paper.

These two examples are the method behind every cis number in Part VI. The per-locus cis-strengths of Section 6.5, the proxy-disagreement result, and the thresholds that drive the version-3 simulation are all produced by exactly this procedure: take matched regions, count exact shared words in sliding windows, separate gene from landscape, and read the conserved enhancers. It uses no alignment heuristics and no tunable scores, so it is deterministic and a reader can reproduce every figure to the digit.

Why this reading differs from the standard one

This framework reaches conclusions that differ from several widely held readings of how genomes make organisms. Because the framework is new, we owe the reader an explicit, fair account of where we differ and why — and we settle each difference by measurement, not by assertion. We are not disputing anyone’s data; we look at the same genomes. What differs is which layer we read as the species-distinguishing information. In each pair below we state the standard reading, our reading, the reason, and the evidence, and we note where the standard reading is right within its range. The honest summary is that the standard readings are not wrong so much as aimed at the wrong layer: they describe the parts, while the species-distinguishing information is in the arrangement and the early cis setting.

What makes a species

How form is set

What an enhancer is, over evolutionary time

How to measure cis

Why two species cannot interbreed

The standard account here has a name, and it is worth stating fully because we build on it rather than against it. The Dobzhansky–Muller model explains reproductive isolation as the accumulation of incompatible mutations: two populations diverge, each fixing changes that work fine on its own genetic background but clash when combined in a hybrid, so the hybrid is sterile or inviable. It is a powerful and well-supported idea, and nothing in our data contradicts it. What we add is structure: we name two concrete, measurable kinds of incompatibility and argue that a pair must clear both, which is why a single divergence number never ordered the outcomes.

Where the body plan comes from

Where size lives, and what non-coding DNA is for

Upgrades to earlier claims, made explicit

Version 6’s measurements revise several statements made in earlier Parts. We list the revisions so that the record is transparent; each is a sharpening or a correction, not a reversal of the framework.

  • Form is more than the Hox map. Part V read form largely through the colinear Hox arrangement. Version 6 generalizes: form is a conserved circuit read by a cis setting, of which the Hox axial map is one instance among many (limb, tail, tooth, digit, eye, skin, jaw, lung). The Hox result stands; it is now a case of the broader rule.

  • cis is a setting with a value, not a bare on/off. Part V’s telomerase example framed a cis element as a switch that is on or off. Version 6’s ZRS measurement shows the setting is quantitative: the snake’s enhancer is weakened, not deleted (conserved core, diverged flanks). “Switch” becomes “switch with a graded setting.”

  • The telomerase switch was one case of a general rule. What Part V showed at a single locus (TERT) — the genome supplies the switch, the cell sets the value — version 6 establishes broadly, with direct measurement, across limb, tail, and the early developmental loci.

  • The continuous value is late cis and runtime — not runtime alone. Part V’s decisive elephant test showed the size value is not in the architecture (a human and an elephant ∼48×\sim\!48\times apart in mass build the same switch shape) and glossed this as “the value is runtime.” Version 6’s measurements refine it in both directions: the late growth cis is the most divergent layer (8.3×8.3\times less conserved human–mouse), but a direct 48×48\times human–elephant test (Section 11.5) finds that growth cis at the conservation noise floor — so it is the least-constrained layer, not a recoverable store of the magnitude. The architecture-refutation stands; and the original “value is runtime/regulatory” reading is largely vindicated against the interim gloss “value is in the growth cis,” which we withdraw. Where the magnitude lives is not resolved by conservation.

  • The conservation law gains a depth axis. Part II’s “importance predicts fixedness” now extends into the cis layer with a depth statement: the earlier in the developmental cascade a cis element acts, the more deeply it is fixed, because everything downstream is built on it.

  • Two layers become three. Part IV’s part–arrangement decoupling (parts shared, arrangement reorganized) gains a third level: parts (shared) ⊂\subset arrangement (reorganized between clades) ⊂\subset early cis setting (the species-defining layer).

New propositions and hypotheses

We register version 6’s claims in the paper’s two registers — rules (present-tense regularities we measured) and hypotheses (testable proposals with stated falsification conditions). Each hypothesis names what would refute it, because a proposal that nothing could refute is not science.

Rules (measured).

  • R20 (conserved circuit, cis setting). For a trait that differs between species, the building circuit is conserved single-copy and the difference is a cis setting. Evidence: growth and morphogen circuits single-copy across cat/tiger/mouse/human/dog/python; the limbless python retains 9/99/9 core limb genes. Status: verified.

  • R21 (retained-but-tuned enhancer). A lost or reduced body feature can leave its enhancer retained but weakened, not deleted. Evidence: python ZRS shares 112112 exact 1515-mers with human versus 11 for control; conserved core, diverged flanks. Status: [INADMISSIBLE — Article IV: pattern-derived, not interpretation](pattern: shared-kk-mer counting).

  • R22 (early masters are cis landscapes). The earliest developmental master genes are tiny genes in vast non-coding landscapes. Evidence: 9191–99%99\% non-coding over their loci (GSC 99.4%99.4\%). Status: verified (coordinate/length; restored — read from annotation, not similarity).

  • R23 (early enhancers conserved in existence). Top-of-cascade enhancers are conserved between species of different form. Evidence: SIX3 2828, OTX2 4848 conserved human–mouse enhancers. Status: [INADMISSIBLE — Article IV: pattern-derived, not interpretation](pattern: conserved-enhancer counting).

  • R24 (a proxy can invert). A sequence proxy (CpG density) for enhancer strength can be inverted relative to direct conservation measurement; direct measurement overrules it. Evidence: OTX2 1.001.00 measured versus 0.120.12 proxy; NODAL the reverse. Status: [INADMISSIBLE — Article IV: pattern-derived, not interpretation](pattern: conservation vs CpG-density proxy).

Hypotheses (testable).

  • H-cis1 (early cis defines the species). The cis state of top-of-cascade switches is the species-defining layer. Predicts: closely related species that differ in body plan will differ measurably in early cis state. Refuted if: body- plan differences are found to track protein-coding change with early cis held constant, or if early cis varies freely within a species without phenotypic consequence.

  • H-cis2 (two-barrier interbreeding). Interbreeding requires both chromosome pairing (arrangement) and agreement of early cis state. Predicts: among pairs that pass the arrangement barrier, those that cannot interbreed will differ at a top-of-cascade cis switch. Refuted if: interbreeding outcome is fully predicted by arrangement alone across a panel of pairs, leaving no role for early cis state.

  • H-cis3 (early-cis depth law). The earlier in the cascade a cis element acts, the lower its within-species variation and the higher its cross-species conservation. Predicts: a monotone relationship between cascade depth and fixedness. Refuted if: late-acting cis elements are found as conserved and invariant as the earliest ones. Supported? [INADMISSIBLE — Article IV: pattern-derived, not interpretation](pattern: shared-kk-mer conservation ratio) — the early organizer cis measured 8.3×8.3\times more conserved (human–mouse) than the late growth cis (IGF1/IGF2/HMGA2), by the same method on the same species pair (Section 11) — the late layer is the divergent one, as the law predicts.

  • H-form (circuit++cis universality). Every between-species form difference decomposes into a conserved circuit plus a cis setting. Predicts: for any reduced or altered feature, the circuit will be found retained and the difference cis-regulatory. Refuted if: a form difference is found to require the gain or loss of a core circuit gene rather than a cis change.

  • H-emergence (self-organized body plan). Body axes and regional domains self-organize from conserved circuits read by cis thresholds, rather than being specified position-by- position. Predicts: minimal circuit++threshold models reproduce axis and domain formation and respond to single-threshold changes (as our simulation shows). Refuted if: axis formation is shown to require position-specific instructions not reducible to such circuits.

Discussion: one mechanism across scales

Pulled together, versions 1–6 tell a single story that descends through levels and arrives at one mechanism. At the level of bulk sequence, a genome specifies position and local shape, while the cell sets quantity (Parts I–II). At the level of arrangement across genomes, the parts are shared and what distinguishes higher taxa is how the command architecture is organized and centralized (Part IV). At the level of form and lifespan, the body is a locked core plus an expandable surface, and a cis switch like telomerase’s is set at runtime (Part V). And at the level this version reaches — the cis-regulatory sequence itself (Part VI) — size and form turn out to be the same kind of object: a conserved circuit read by a cis setting, with the earliest settings the most deeply fixed and therefore the most truly species-defining.

The throughline is the two-layer asymmetry stated on the first page: the genome encodes position and wiring strongly, and quantity weakly, leaving quantity to the runtime. A cis setting is exactly where those two layers touch. Its disposition — that an enhancer exists, where it sits, which gene it reads — is Layer 1, written in the sequence and conserved. Its value — how strongly it fires, and so how big a limb, how long a tail, how large a forebrain — is the regulated quantity, the knob. Evolution between related species, on this reading, is mostly the turning of these knobs on a shared machine, with the deepest knobs turned most rarely because everything is built on them.

This is why we could measure it. A knob that is a sequence is a knob you can read. The snake did not throw away its limb machine; it turned the limb knob down, and the turned-down knob is still legible in its DNA, conserved core and diverged flanks. The forebrain enhancers that a human and a mouse share are the same machine with the knobs set differently. And when we put conserved circuits and cis thresholds into a simulation, a body plan organized itself, and turning one knob changed the form — which is the whole claim, made to run.

We have been careful to mark the edges. Circuit presence we measured directly and broadly; cis conservation we measured directly but for two species and at the loci where we obtained sequence; the developmental-barrier account of interbreeding and the species-defining role of early cis are interpretations with stated tests; the simulation is a sufficiency demonstration, not a quantitative model. The immediate next experiments are concrete and within reach: run the simulation under human versus mouse measured cis to reproduce a species form difference; measure early cis states across a panel of interbreeding-tested pairs to test the two-barrier hypothesis; and extend the enhancer-conservation measurement to more species to test the depth law. Each is falsifiable, and each follows the same discipline as everything before it: read the sequence, measure what it says, and let the measurement — not the proxy, not the prior, not the consensus — decide.

Reproducibility map (version 6)

Reproducibility is the heart of this paper, and version 6 holds to the same contract as the earlier versions: every number above is recomputable from inputs we name, by scripts we ship, with the method stated. This section is the map. It lists, for each result, the input sequence, the script, the method, and the output, so that a reader can rebuild any figure from scratch. The bundle is provided separately and is kept under 250250 MB; the inputs are region-level FASTA, not whole genomes, precisely so that the bundle stays small and the regulatory regions stay legible.

Inputs (region-level FASTA, extracted from public assemblies)

All sequence was taken as regions (not whole genomes) from the public assemblies through the NCBI Genome Data Viewer, each region specified as accession:start–end so that it is exactly reproducible. The regions:

  • Human SHH–LMBR1 locus (ZRS): NC_000007.14:155799980–156893183 (1,093,2041{,}093{,}204 bp). Contains SHH, LMBR1, and the ZRS in the LMBR1 intron.

  • Python LMBR1 region (ZRS): NW_006534369.1:1–150000 (150,000150{,}000 bp, scaffold-level; carries LMBR1).

  • Human early loci (∼300\sim\!300 kb each): SIX3 NC_000002.12:44791702–45096071; POU5F1 NC_000006.12:31014337–31320682; SOX2 NC_000003.12:181561925–181864436; NODAL NC_000010.11:70281936–70597951; GSC NC_000014.9:94618223–94920113; OTX2 NC_000014.9:56649905–56960479; PAX6 NC_000011.10:31639026–31967961; FOXA2 NC_000020.11:22430998–22735490.

  • Mouse early loci (∼300\sim\!300 kb each): SIX3 NC_000083.7:85771036–86083619; POU5F1 NC_000083.7:35666929–35971674 (carries a ∼50\sim\!50 kb assembly gap); SOX2 NC_000069.7:34554144–34856610; NODAL NC_000076.7:61103751–61411117; GSC NC_000078.7:104287468–104589495; OTX2 NC_000080.7:48744238–49055101; PAX6 NC_000068.8:105349241–105678755; FOXA2 NC_000068.8:147734797–148038889.

  • Growth-cis loci (∼320\sim\!320–440440 kb each), human and mouse: IGF1 NC_000012.12:102245874–102631839 (human) / NC_000076.7:87544127–87922909 (mouse); IGF2 NC_000011.10:1979117–2299566 / NC_000073.7:142054505–142370566; HMGA2 NC_000012.12:65674460–66116291 / NC_000076.7:120047173–120462374. Used to measure where the size difference is stored (Section 11).

  • Gene tables (coordinates, the core small data). The arrangement and circuit-presence analyses run on per-gene coordinate tables (one row per gene: name, chromosome/scaffold, start, end, strand, biotype), which are tiny compared with sequence and are shipped in full. Two groups: (a) session-supplied — the assemblies the author provided across sessions: chimpanzee, tiger, lion, horse, donkey, dog, fox, pigeon, python, axolotl, Takifugu (fugu), platypus, sheep, goat, cow, and cat; (b) core — the tables used for the cross-species circuit checks: human, mouse, rat, chicken, Anolis, Xenopus, zebrafish, amphioxus, sea urchin, Nematostella, and Drosophila. All are in the bundle under gene_tables_session/ and gene_tables_core/.

  • E. coli pangenome tables. The fourteen E. coli strain gene tables behind the pangenome/core-genome analysis are shipped under ecoli_pangenome/.

Scripts and methods

Each result maps to a named script and an explicit method.

  • ZRS conservation (R21). Method: count exact shared 1515-mers between human ZRS windows and the python LMBR1 region; controls = LMBR1 promoter, random intron, SHH exon; within-ZRS coverage map for conserved-core/diverged-flank. Output: 112112 vs 77 vs 11 vs 00; 18%18\% core coverage. Why 1515-mers: an exact 1515-mer match is ∼10−9\sim\!10^{-9} by chance in 150150 kb, so an excess is real homology, not coincidence.

  • Circuit presence (R20, R22 circuits, eight features, T1–T10). Method: match circuit gene names against each species’ protein-coding annotation, applying organism-native names (fish pou5f3, ta/ntl, her1/her7; teleost a/b paralogs); never declare a loss before the native-name check. Output: python 9/99/9 limb; human 11/1111/11 tail; eight-feature presence table; T1–T10 conserved across six species.

  • Non-coding fraction (R22). Method: (locus length −- gene-body length)//locus length, gene body from the gene table. Output: 9191–99%99\% per early locus.

  • SIX3 enhancers and per-locus cis-strength (R23). Method: 500500-bp sliding windows on the human locus, exact shared 1515-mers with the mouse locus per window; peaks above background, excluding the coding body, are conserved non-coding enhancers; sum the peak counts for a per-locus cis-strength, normalized 00–11. Output: SIX3 2828 enhancers, total 4,3414{,}341; eight-locus cis-strength vector.

  • Proxy disagreement (R24). Method: compute CpG density (and CpG observed/ expected) per locus; compare normalized to the measured conservation. Output: the two vectors disagree (e.g. OTX2 1.001.00 measured vs 0.120.12 proxy).

  • Size storage: growth cis vs early cis (Section 11). Script: growth_vs_early_cis.py. Method: measure human–mouse conserved-enhancer signal for the growth loci (IGF1/IGF2/HMGA2) by the same sliding-window shared-1515-mer method as the early loci, and compare. Output: growth mean 407407 vs early mean 34013401 (8.3×8.3\times); shared-fraction 0.00550.0055 vs 0.02150.0215 — the growth cis is the divergent layer where size is stored.

  • cis four-dimensional decomposition (Section 5). Method: from each species’ gene table, extract the SHH–LMBR1 neighbourhood; record gene order (D1, D4), same-chromosome linkage and SHH–LMBR1 spacing (D2), and fractional positions of SHH and LMBR1 (D3). Output: identical neighbourhood order in human/mouse/chicken/cat; SHH–LMBR1 fractional gap ≤0.005\le 0.005 while absolute position ranges 0.050.05–0.990.99.

  • Simulation (H-emergence; Section 8). Scripts: devo_sim.py (v1, hand-set thresholds), devo_sim_v2.py (thresholds from CpG density, with left-right axis), devo_sim_v3.py (thresholds from measured human–mouse conservation), and devo_sim_humanmouse.py (the paired run: the same circuit under each species’ own measured cis). Method: the algorithm of the development-simulation listing; outputs are the field arrays, the domain map, the axis metrics, and the single-threshold sweep. Output: axis and domain formation; forebrain 26%→37%→49%26\%\!\to\!37\%\!\to\!49\% under one threshold; v3 metrics with measured cis.

Bundle manifest (kept under 250 MB)

The version 6 additions to the reproducibility bundle:

  • Sequences: the eighteen region FASTA files listed above (human and mouse early loci, the human SHH–LMBR1 locus, the python LMBR1 region). These are regions, not genomes, so the total is small.

  • Gene tables (the core small data): gene_tables_session/ (the 1717 author-supplied assemblies: chimp, tiger, lion, horse, donkey, dog, fox, pigeon, python, axolotl, fugu, platypus, sheep, goat, cow, cat, and a duplicate dog) and gene_tables_core/ (the 1111 reference tables: human, mouse, rat, chicken, Anolis, Xenopus, zebrafish, amphioxus, urchin, Nematostella, fly). ecoli_pangenome/ holds the 1414 E. coli strain tables. These are the inputs to every arrangement and circuit-presence result across all versions, and they are small (coordinate rows, not sequence).

  • Derived values (JSON): cis_strength_measured.json (measured human–mouse cis-strength), cis_strength_cpg.json (the CpG proxy), six3_cis_measured.json (per-enhancer SIX3), cis_strengths.json (motif-density features), v6_numbers.json (the consolidated figures cited in this Part).

  • Simulation: devo_sim.py, devo_sim_v2.py, devo_sim_v3.py, devo_sim_humanmouse.py, and the four figure files (devo_sim_figure.png, devo_sim_v2_figure.png, devo_sim_v3_figure.png, devo_sim_humanmouse_figure.png); plus the per-species measured cis (cis_strength_byspecies.json) and the paired-run output (sim_humanmouse.json); and the size-storage measurement (growth_vs_early_cis.py, growth_vs_early_cis.json, growth_vs_early_figure.png), which compares growth cis to early cis.

  • Method note: every script uses only numpy/scipy and the standard library; the 1515-mer measurements use exact string matching (no alignment heuristics), so they are deterministic and platform-independent.

Reproducibility map (version 7): the session’s new analyses

Version 7 adds a set of lineage-controlled size tests, two switch models, and a form/coordinate study, under the same contract. This is their map: each result, the in-paper listing of its algorithm, the script, and the output. Full input hashes and fetch commands are in the bundle’s DATA_LOCK; the small received sequences ship directly (with a CONTENTS file giving each one’s region and role), and the whole version-7 addition stays well under 250250 MB.

New inputs (region-level FASTA, accession:start–end). Cat growth-plate loci — IHH NC_058375.1:202824483–203030931, NPR2 NC_058380.1:57374786–57591542, FGFR3 NC_058371.1:204203604–204420499, PTHLH NC_058374.1:61069608–61283905, SOX9 NC_058381.1:52970295–53175102 — and the matching tiger loci (IHH NC_056667.1:201230264–201436600; NPR2 NC_056672.1:56983701–57200353; FGFR3 NC_056663.1:203082832–203298066; PTHLH NC_056666.1:60390915–60602776; SOX9 NC_056673.1:52502210–52706987). The let-7 test reads HMGA2 mRNA NM_010441.3 (mouse) and the cat genomic 3’UTR NC_058374.1:91115000–91128000 (the cat mRNA XM_023257202.2 is 3’UTR-truncated and excluded from the count); the elephant growth-plate loci are from assembly mLoxAfr1 (RUNX2/IHH/NPR2/FGFR3/PTHLH/SOX9); the dosage circuit reads the public GFF annotations named in DATA_LOCK; the cat/tiger chr-D1/C2/A2 paired loci are the size-contrast controls. The Hox A–P coordinates are derived once from the human HOXA order into hox_ap.json.

Version-7 reproducibility map: result →\to in-paper algorithm →\to script →\to output.
Result (paper item) Algorithm Script Output
Result Algorithm Script Output
Core A4 pipeline (E. coli gate) §6 key_pipeline_full.py 470470/471471/45064506/90129012; gates pass
Dosage across ∼8000×\sim\!8000\times (Test A) the dosage-test listing dosage.py growth/hormone/appetite single-copy
Regulatory density, within-family (Test B) the CpG-test listing crossspecies.py, decisive.py, arch.py mouse–elephant 5/55/5; cat–tiger 1/51/5
let-7/HMGA2 stop-coordinate the let-7/HMGA2 listing let7_hmga2.py 8/8/88/8/8; mouse 1010
Condensate switch the conserved-switch listing stiffness_switch.py ds/dϕ≈438\mathrm{d}s/\mathrm{d}\phi\approx438; hysteresis
Rb–E2F response switch the response-switch listing response_switch.py bistable; S*≈0.66S^\ast\approx0.66
Form: pattern ⊥\perp size — devo_morphogenesis.py, dna_to_form.py proportions size-invariant
Hox limb-coordinate (tetrapod) the Hox-limb listing dna_to_animal.py ++ hox_ap.json fore 0.520.52, hind 0.770.77
Elephant attempt (trunk == variable) — build_elephant.py short nose when fair; long trunk only hand-set
Read a locus’s cis load — read_cis.py per-locus CpG/A4

New metafiles. DATA_LOCK (3939 inputs: accession, fetch, SHA-256), MANIFEST (output hashes), gate_verdicts.csv (one verdict per result), env.txt (interpreter and library versions with run commands), and the method logs METHOD_LOG.md and DICTIONARY_ADDITIONS.md (the dictionary entries of Section 20 with their runnable referents). The appetite circuit is counted inside dosage.py (no separate file), and build_elephant.py first runs dna_to_animal.py to cache the body.

DNA grammar dictionary (quick reference)

A terse distillation of the framework, for quick reference; the body (Sections 2–3.4) expands each item.

Objects (A4). shell (stiffness segment) ⋅\cdot anchor (fixed boundary or recognized position) ⋅\cdot motor (machine site) ⋅\cdot loop (3D contact). Position in the sequence is a coordinate on the helix (3.43.4 Å and 34.29∘34.29^\circ per base pair).

Grammars. G1 physics: GC →\to stable/stiff; AT and poly-A tracts →\to soft/open. G2 recognition: a short pattern →\to a protein binds. Everything else commonly called a “rule” is one of these applied to a different cis value.

Dynamics. INIT →\to SCONSERV (satisfy hard constraints, conservative) →\to SDISSIP (relax, dissipative) →\to JEVENT (irreversible cut/bond/lock). Gates: an irreversible event releases energy (ΔE<0\Delta E<0); residual stress does not rise during relaxation.

Selection. Among the configurations a region admits, the one realized minimizes residual stress PresP_{\mathrm{res}} subject to hard constraints; ordering matters, not the absolute value; lowest-in-set is not global-optimum.

Parameters and scope. A value is cis (in the sequence) or trans/environmental (set by the cell). Scope runs local →\to regional →\to global →\to individual; a global value is invisible to per-site regression and appears only in whole-genome fitting.

Variability. Fixed (constant, carries no variation), cis (sequence-set), environmental (cell-set), genetic×\timesenvironment (both, hardest). What is fixed is relative to scope; the scope of first variation names the biological unit.

The asymmetry. Position is strongly sequence-encoded (Layer 1); quantity is set by the runtime, global resources, and copy numbers the sequence may not contain (Layer 2).
