Conventions. Composition over called bases; width-independent nearest-site matching; recognition motifs learned within folds; AT-matched negatives.

Arrangement across genomes (v4). A second dictionary, in Section 2, names the cross-genome arrangement words: compact/pure colinear cluster, cluster split/dispersal, multiplied clean cluster, bidirectional shared-promoter pair, frozen microsynteny unit, free-rearrangement background, chromosomal linkage, rearrangement rate, tandem array, broadcast-receptor infrastructure, local specialized switch, command-architecture axis, part–arrangement decoupling, module consolidation, broadcast-channel depth, centralization completeness, heat-mode switch, and controller-singular/sensor-free. Their shared meaning: arrangement persists only where central control forbids rearrangement, so shared arrangement measures the completeness of centralization.

cis measured from sequence (v6). A further set of arrangement words, in Section 2 and built on direct sequence in Part : conserved trait-control circuit, morphogen circuit (form ≠\neq parts), cis decomposed by arrangement (host/linkage/anchor/order), circuit-kept-form-switched-off, the early-development cascade (T1–T10), the two mating barriers, the graded cis setting (a knob, not a toggle), the tiny gene in a vast cis landscape, the enhancer conserved in existence, the early-cis depth law, proxy inversion (measurement overrules a correlate), the multi-function species vector, and the methodological rule of native-name reading. Their shared meaning: a trait is a conserved circuit read by a cis setting; the genome supplies the circuit (the reader), and the cis value is the knob — with the earliest, top-of-cascade settings the most deeply fixed and therefore species-defining. cis is thus not a bare on/off but a setting with a graded value (the snake’s limb enhancer is weakened, not deleted), and where the sequence is in hand the value is measurable (Part ).

Size, switch, and form read (v7). A final set of words, built on direct sequence and seeded simulation in this version: the local/global rule (R18 — form is a local arrangement, the size value a global parameter, the split scale-invariant); the conserved response switch (R19 — sequence sets the threshold, runtime flips the state, with hysteresis); target →\to rate →\to stiffness (R25, hypothesis — a global size target imposed via growth rate/duration and coupled back to stiffness by Hippo/YAP mechanotransduction); the within-family control (a cross-species size signal counts only if it replicates within a family — mouse–elephant 5/55/5 fails at cat–tiger 1/51/5, so it was lineage, not size); the growth-brake stop-coordinate (let-7 sites in the HMGA2 3’UTR, conserved 8/8/88/8/8, mouse 1010); single-copy dosage invariance (the growth/hormone/appetite circuit is single-copy across ∼8000×\sim\!8000\times mass); organ length is the variable layer (a genome read fairly gives the body plan but not the elephant’s long trunk — position is a coordinate, length is runtime); Hox limb-coordinate reading (limb positions read from Hox colinearity, fore ≈0.52\approx 0.52 / hind ≈0.77\approx 0.77); and the equal-length-Hamming artifact (truncating to equal length fakes divergence — align first; the GDF15/IRS4 claim was retracted). Their shared meaning: position and form are readable local coordinates, while size is a global, distributed value — established not by finding it but by lineage-controlled nulls that show where it is not; the switch is the amplifier, and how the value is physically imposed remains an explicit hypothesis. Sections 12–10.

Reproducibility. Lock–Derive–Gate; core pipeline in the paper; analysis scripts and hashes in the bundle (Appendix 2); the version 6 region FASTA, derived JSON, and simulation scripts are mapped in Section 19 and kept under 250250 MB.

Version 8: consolidating the dictionary into a self-gating pipeline

Versions 4–7 built the two-layer reading and a working pipeline; version 8 turns it into a finished, self-checking DNA interpreter and retires the last hand-set numbers. The result is a single pipeline that reads a genome the way the theory prescribes: it computes position and form from the sequence (Layer 1) and treats quantity as the runtime’s job (Layer 2), deterministically, with every step checking its own gates. Version 8 establishes four things. First, the central knob of the stiffness signal — the GC weight — is no longer chosen: it is decoded from nearest-neighbour thermodynamics and then confirmed on measured data, on three independent lines of evidence. Second, rigidity has a mechanism: the sequence sets a condensate’s shape, and a runtime-built calcium shell makes it rigid — one principle running from a molecular droplet to an eggshell and a growth plate. Third, the theory’s sharpest prediction holds where it is hardest to fake: across six species and roughly a million-fold range of body size, the sequence- and protein-level knobs are conserved and carry no size signal — size is the runtime, as claimed. Fourth, every function is tied to a dictionary entry, an R-rule, and a status (decoded / grounded / Layer-2), so the hand-tuned surface falls from eight knobs to zero. What remains genuinely open is small and named — two fine-scale chromatin questions, each with its next experiment — and the external data are pinned by accession and SHA-256. The rest of this section walks through each result.

The parameter ledger

The consolidation tracks each knob of the pipeline and its disposition. “Fully un-decoded” (a hand-set number with no referent) falls 8→5→2→08\!\to\!5\!\to\!2\!\to\!0 across the work.

Knob Source Status
A4.W, A4.step locked window/step decoded
A4.w_gc NN stacking-stability slope (+1.10+1.10 kcal/mol per GC) decoded
A4.w_cpg CpG-step stability (++ sign validated) grounded
A4.w_at A-tract nucleosome exclusion grounded
cis.load read_cis composition read decoded
4d.reader_gain conserved single-copy GH/IGF circuit decoded
4d.material_supply runtime nutrient menu (N, energy, Ca, P) Layer-2
4d.t_stop let-7/HMGA2 timing Layer-2
switch.gamma sequence γ\gamma (NN stacking stability) decoded
switch.phi_c runtime fill (operating point) Layer-2

“Decoded” means the hand-tuning is closed, not that the value is independently measured; the switch and the size integrator remain illustrative-model tier (their threshold/structure is read from sequence, but they are not separately calibrated).

The GC weight, decoded and then confirmed

The “GC weight” of the stiffness signal is not a chosen 1.01.0: it is the slope of GC against duplex stacking stability computed from the SantaLucia (1998) unified nearest-neighbour free energies. On mouse Ihh the stability is +0.84+1.10GC+0.84 + 1.10\,\mathrm{GC} (coefficient of determination 0.9870.987), so the weight is the physics slope +1.10+1.10 kcal/mol per unit GC. This decode generalises: across 5151 regions spanning six species and four functional classes (growth-plate, early-development master genes, enhancers/limb, systemic-growth loci) the GC→\tostacking fit holds with R2>0.96R^{2}>0.96 at every locus, and the A4 pipeline’s self-gates pass on all 5151. Cross-species γ\gamma at the same locus is conserved (cat versus tiger, across a 50×50\times body-mass difference, |Δγ|=0.002|\Delta\gamma|=0.002), consistent with γ\gamma being set by sequence composition rather than size. Finally, the same weight’s sign is confirmed on measured data: regressing an in-vivo MNase nucleosome-occupancy map (Tsankov et al. 2010) on sequence over 23,09423{,}094 windows gives a strong positive GC coefficient (standardised βGC=+4.06\beta_{\mathrm{GC}}=+4.06, t=+27t=+27). The GC weight thus rests on three independent legs: thermodynamics, cross-species generalisation, and measured occupancy.

The stiffness shell, made explicit

A bare condensate droplet is held round by interfacial tension γ\gamma (sequence-set, Layer 1) but is fragile: it resists deformation only through a small Laplace stress ∼γ/R\sim\gamma/R and ruptures on contact. Surface tension gives shape, not rigidity. Rigidity appears when a structured shell forms at the interface — a pH\mathrm{pH}/acid–base–controlled mineral deposit (calcium carbonate or phosphate) or a cross-linked layer — with a thin-shell buckling stress ∼kE(t/R)2g(c)\sim k\,E\,(t/R)^{2}\,g(c), where g(c)g(c) has a percolation threshold near c*=0.5c^{*}\!=\!0.5 because only a connected membrane bears load. In the consolidated model the bare droplet sits near 10210^{2} Pa while a closed calcium shell reaches ∼3×104\sim\!3\times10^{4} Pa — about 300×300\times more robust — and increasing γ\gamma alone (even tenfold) cannot approach the shelled value. The genome (Layer 1) sets the soft phase and its composition (γ\gamma); the runtime (Layer 2) supplies the structuring material (calcium, phosphate) and triggers the deposition that closes the shell, so the shell/boundary is partly emergent — matching the predicted-negative result that compartment boundaries are not sequence-placed (area-under-curve ≈0.52\approx 0.52). The same thin-shell law scales from a micron condensate to the eggshell and the amniotic membrane, and to growth-plate endochondral mineralisation (cartilage hardened into bone by calcium phosphate); these are one principle — soft interior plus structured surface equals rigidity — across scales (the scale-invariance rule). This also explains why, in the response switch, higher γ\gamma yields a more robust (not steeper) bistability: barrier height ∝γ2\propto\gamma^{2} and hysteresis width ∝γ3/2\propto\gamma^{3/2}, because a structured shell resists deformation.

The response switch and the size integral

The R19 condensate switch F(s;h)=14s4−12γs2−hsF(s;h)=\tfrac14 s^{4}-\tfrac12\gamma s^{2}-hs no longer carries free numbers: its barrier is the sequence-read γ\gamma (so the same nearest-neighbour physics that decodes the GC weight sets the switch threshold), and ϕc\phi_{c} is identified as the Layer-2 fill (the runtime operating point). The size model is written as SIZE=∫(reader_gain[sequence]×material_supply[Layer-2])dt\mathrm{SIZE}=\int \big(\,\text{reader\_gain}[\text{sequence}] \times \text{material\_supply}[\text{Layer-2}]\,\big)\,dt: the genome encodes the readers (a conserved, single-copy growth-and-appetite circuit), the environment supplies the limited materials, and size is their runtime integral — which is why every local size probe returned conserved. Both remain illustrative-model tier.

The finite materials

Two finite “materials” are catalogued, one per scale. Material 1 (molecular): the ten unique nearest-neighbour stacking free energies (SantaLucia 1998), which give γ\gamma and decode the GC weight, ground the CpG and A-tract terms, and set the switch threshold. Material 2 (organism): the limited body-building inputs (amino-acid nitrogen, energy, calcium, phosphate), read by the conserved circuit; here the stiffness-shell mechanism upgrades calcium and phosphate from mere “growth fuel” to the shell-structuring material that converts the soft, γ\gamma-shaped phase into a rigid shell. Both obey one law: a finite material, read by a conserved reader, sets a global quantity not stored at any single locus.

Larger animals: γ\gamma across ∼2×105\sim\!2\times10^{5}-fold body mass

The cat-versus-tiger pair (∼50×\sim\!50\times mass) is extended to four species spanning about 200,000×200{,}000\times — mouse (∼0.025\sim\!0.025 kg), cat (∼4\sim\!4 kg), tiger (∼220\sim\!220 kg), elephant (∼5000\sim\!5000 kg) — on the five orthologous growth-plate loci IHH, FGFR3, NPR2, PTHLH, SOX9 (biggest_animals_v8.py). The sequence-set stiffness γ\gamma is conserved (mean within-orthologue coefficient of variation 2.0%2.0\%, below the between-gene 3.4%3.4\%) and, decisively, does not track body size: after removing each gene’s mean, the correlation of γ\gamma with log10\log_{10} body mass is +0.002+0.002. Individual loci show only weak, opposite-signed trends (FGFR3 up, PTHLH down, neither significant on four points) that cancel, and the largest single spread (NPR2) is a felid-lineage offset, not a size effect. By Article IV this is not a similarity argument; the admissible result is the absence of a size signal in the knob. A 200,000×200{,}000\times size difference leaves γ\gamma where it is — exactly what “size is Layer 2 (runtime), not written at these loci” predicts.

The protein intermediate

The genome does not act on structure directly: it is transcribed and translated, and proteins are the agents in between. Making this explicit sharpens the two-layer reading at three nodes. (i) The readers of the size machinery — the GH/IGF and appetite circuit (e.g. GHRHR, POU1F1, IGF1/2) — are proteins, so the “reader gain” of the size integral is a protein’s activity. (ii) The shell-builders are proteins: RUNX2 (the master mineralisation factor), SOX9 (the master chondrocyte factor), the IHH/PTHLH signalling proteins that set the mineralisation front, and the enzymes and scaffolds (alkaline phosphatase, collagen) that deposit and structure the calcium shell — so the stiffness-shell mechanism is protein-executed: the runtime calcium (Layer 2) is laid down by protein machinery encoded in Layer 1. (iii) Tissue is protein, so size is accumulated protein: the Layer-2 material (amino-acid nitrogen, energy, Ca, P) becomes protein, and SIZE=∫(reader gain)×(material→protein synthesis)dt\mathrm{SIZE}=\int(\text{reader gain})\times(\text{material}\!\to\!\text{protein synthesis})\,dt. This explains in one move why form is conserved while size varies: the same proteins (hence the conserved γ\gamma measured above), built up in larger or smaller amount as the runtime material allows. Reading the proteomes directly (protein_v8.py) confirms this across six species from mouse to the humpback whale — about 1,200,000×1{,}200{,}000\times body mass — with the best-annotated reference, Homo sapiens, included as a final check. On the six growth-plate proteins, length is conserved (coefficient of variation 3%3\%) and the condensate-relevant sticker composition (aromatic plus charged residues — a protein-level γ\gamma proxy) is tightly conserved (within-orthologue 1.5%1.5\% against a between-gene 10.4%10.4\%; SOX9 varies by 0.2%0.2\% across all six); after removing each protein’s mean, its correlation with log10\log_{10} body mass is +0.05+0.05. Adding the largest animal had earlier removed a weak four-species lean (rr had been +0.27+0.27), and the human reference leaves the null intact: there is no size code. The protein — the conserved form — is Layer 1; how much of it accumulates is Layer 2. The conserved DNA γ\gamma above is the shadow of conserved proteins.

The open frontier, and a method that corrects itself

A finished interpreter earns trust by being exact about its frontier. Two fine-scale chromatin questions are deliberately left open, each with a precise next experiment that would settle it. (i) The A-tract term w_at is not promoted. Regressed against the in-vivo MNase occupancy, the A-tract marginal effect after controlling for GC is essentially zero (partial correlation +0.007+0.007); aligned on A-tract centres, short tracts (≥6\ge 6) show a mild depletion (0.81×0.81\times) but longer tracts are spuriously enriched (≥10:1.26×\ge 10\!:\!1.26\times, ≥15:2.68×\ge 15\!:\!2.68\times, ≥20:3.64×\ge 20\!:\!3.64\times), the opposite of exclusion and with the wrong dose-response. This is the signature of MNase’s A/T cleavage bias compounded by a handful of rare long-tract loci, not a refutation of the physics. w_at therefore stays grounded-in-literature. (ii) The ∼10\sim\!10 bp positioning periodicity is present at the correct period in the WW power spectrum (10.1410.14 bp) but only 1.14×1.14\times above the local baseline, and the dyad-aligned profile smears because in-vivo MNase peaks are not base-pair-precise. Both converge on the same need: a base-pair-precise chemical (Brogaard) nucleosome-dyad map in the same (R64/sacCer3) build as the sequence. With that single small file, both tests can be done cleanly; with in-vivo MNase they cannot.

The pipeline is built to catch its own mistakes, and four are recorded in the open rather than quietly fixed — which is how it stays trustworthy: a load normalisation artifact in the integrator; a naive “higher γ\gamma gives a sharper switch” claim (the true dependence is more robust, not steeper); a composition bug that divided GC by window length and let N-steps dilute the stacking average, spuriously tanking R2R^{2} on a gap-rich locus (mouse POU5F1, 16%16\% N) and fixed to “composition over called bases”; and a loose-gate false pass in the periodicity test (raw autocorrelation), replaced by a power-spectrum prominence gate.

Provenance (DATA_LOCK, version 8)

Two external datasets are used, both in the R64/sacCer3 build and length-matched on all sixteen chromosomes. The sequence is the yeast assembly GCA_000146045.2 (R64); the measured occupancy is an in-vivo MNase map (Tsankov et al. 2010, GEO GSE21960) as redistributed per-base by the NucleosomeDensity repository, recorded with its commit and SHA-256. The occupancy was never used to build the A4 or γ\gamma, so the GC-weight confirmation is a held-out test.

Reproducibility map (version 8)

Eight annotated modules, with a dictionary, materials catalogue, gate-verdict log, and data lock, reproduce the above. All internal gates pass except the two honest open items.

Module Role Gates
gamma_decode_v8.py GC→\tostacking γ\gamma; decodes w_gc PASS
generalize_v8.py 5151 loci / 66 species / 44 classes PASS (51/5151/51)
switch_decode_v8.py R19 threshold from sequence γ\gamma PASS
shell_model_v8.py stiffness-shell mechanism (∼300×\sim\!300\times) PASS
materials_decode_v8.py nutrient menu →\to size readers PASS (illustrative)
pipeline_v8.py consolidated pipeline, ledger, 4D, switch PASS
w_at_decode_v8.py w_at vs measured occupancy w_gc confirmed; w_at open
periodicity_v8.py ∼10\sim\!10 bp WW power spectrum open (faint)
biggest_animals_v8.py γ\gamma across ∼2×105\sim\!2\times10^{5} body mass PASS
protein_v8.py protein form, 6 species incl. human PASS

The single next datum that would close the two open items is a base-pair-precise chemical nucleosome-dyad map (e.g. the Brogaard unique map of ∼67,543\sim\!67{,}543 positions) in the R64/sacCer3 build.

Reconciliation with earlier versions, and the consolidated reproducibility record

Because this account grew in versions, a few earlier statements should be read through the version-8 results that supersede them; they are listed here rather than silently edited.

  • The stiffness-signal weights. Section 3.2 introduces (wGC,wCpG,wAT)=(1.0,0.5,−0.5)(w_{\mathrm{GC}},w_{\mathrm{CpG}},w_{\mathrm{AT}})=(1.0,0.5,-0.5) and states plainly that they are a modeling choice fixed in the Lock. Version 8 supersedes that status: wGCw_{\mathrm{GC}} is decoded as the GC→\tostacking-stability slope (the 1.01.0 is a normalisation; the physical value is +1.10+1.10 kcal/mol per unit GC), wCpGw_{\mathrm{CpG}} is grounded in the strong CpG-step stacking, and wATw_{\mathrm{AT}} is grounded in A-tract nucleosome exclusion. They are no longer free numbers (the ledger above), though only wGCw_{\mathrm{GC}} is measurement-confirmed.

  • The AT-tract term. The confident reading of the negative AT-tract weight in Section 3.2 is tempered: tested against measured occupancy, wATw_{\mathrm{AT}} could not be promoted (MNase A/T-cleavage bias; partial correlation ≈0\approx 0). It remains grounded-in-literature, not measurement-decoded — an honest open item, not an established weight.

  • “Firmness” of a shell. Section 4.1 calls the interfacial tension γ\gamma “the physical content of a shell’s firmness.” Version 8 refines this into two distinct things: γ\gamma (Layer 1, sequence) sets a droplet’s shape and its fast resistance to a quick poke, but a bare droplet is fragile; durable structural rigidity comes from a runtime-built structured shell (calcium mineralisation or cross-linking; Layer 2) — the eggshell and growth-plate principle. “Firmness” is surface tension; rigidity is the shell.

  • Consolidated reproducibility record. The earlier per-version reproducibility maps (versions 5 and 6) are superseded for this release by the version-8 map above, together with DATA_LOCK_v8.md (every external dataset with accession and SHA-256: the yeast R64 genome, the Tsankov occupancy, and the six proteomes) and MANIFEST_v8.md (every shipped file with its SHA-256). The version-8 modules above, the dictionary, the materials catalogue, and the gate-verdict log are the authoritative, self-gating record; the E. coli and let-7/HMGA2 numbers carried from earlier bundles remain Tier C with their accessions.

Learning the method from zero — a curriculum

The body of this paper, and the Version-7 changelog, are written for a reader building on the framework. This appendix is the on-ramp for everyone else: a staged path from background knowledge to running the method and interpreting a locus. It is deliberately friendly — a plain idea first, the formal version second — because the single hardest idea (that size is in the genome yet not findable in any one place) is hard for experts too.

The one analogy to carry throughout

A genome is like a recipe that builds a specific cake. Where the layers and filling go is written explicitly — you can point to the line — and that is position / form (Layer 1). How big the cake comes out is also fully decided by the recipe, but not by one line: it is the sum of every amount and every baking time. That is size / quantity (Layer 2): in the recipe, but spread across the whole of it, not in a single line you can point to. Almost the entire method is making that one distinction precise and measurable.

Prerequisites, and how to use this

Four light backgrounds suffice: a little molecular biology (genes, transcription, enhancers, copy number); a little intuitive physics (phase separation / droplets, surface tension, a free-energy well, a bistable switch); basic Python (run a script; numpy, scipy, Biopython, matplotlib); and basic genomics (FASTA and GFF files, an accession, sequence alignment / percent identity). Do the stages in order. Each stage states a goal, what to read in this paper, what to run from the bundle, and a self-check.

The ten stages

  1. Stage 0 — Orientation. Install Python and the four libraries; fetch one small FASTA by accession and print its length. Ready when “enhancer,” “phase separation,” “FASTA,” and “run a script” are not scary.

  2. Stage 1 — The two layers. Read: the Introduction and “the two-layer proposition.” Self-check: for any feature, say whether it is position (Layer 1) or quantity (Layer 2) — e.g. which intron hosts an enhancer (1) vs how strongly it fires (2).

  3. Stage 2 — The mechanism. Read: Sections 4–4.1. Run: stiffness_switch.py (Section 13) and watch a bistable switch flip. Self-check: which knob does the sequence set (γ\gamma), which does the runtime set (ϕ\phi), and why does overfilling cause a switch rather than a gradual change?

  4. Stage 3 — From letters to structure. Read: the A4 arrangement, the stiffness signal, and Section 3.5. Self-check: from a sequence’s AT/GC pattern alone, predict roughly where soft/stiff shells and their boundaries fall.

  5. Stage 4 — The pipeline, hands-on. Read: Sections 6 and 7. Run: the core pipeline on E. coli and reproduce 470470 shells / 471471 anchors / 4,5064{,}506 motors / 9,0129{,}012 loops with all gates pass; read the Version-7 windowing fix and understand why short loci once broke it. Self-check: name the five steps and what a gate does.

  6. Stage 5 — Reproducibility. Read: Section 9. Do: verify one result end to end — hash the input against DATA_LOCK, run the script, confirm the gate, hash the output against MANIFEST. Self-check: why is “never tuned after the fact” the rule that gives the numbers their force?

  7. Stage 6 — Quantity, and why size is not in the letters. Read: Sections 2, 11, and the lineage-controlled tests 11.7–11.8. Run: dosage.py, crossspecies.py, decisive.py, and the let-7 count; watch the cat-vs-tiger control kill a tempting “size signal.” Self-check: why did every local search for size come back conserved, and what is the difference between “in the genome” and “localizable to one place”?

  8. Stage 7 — Form. Read: Sections 1, 2, and 10. Run: devo_morphogenesis.py and dna_to_animal.py; swap a hand-set limb coordinate for the Hox-read one and watch a deformity become a tetrapod. Self-check: why does reading the coordinate from the Hox arrangement fix the deformity, and what does the genome give cleanly (order/identity) versus not (absolute size)?

  9. Stage 8 — The synthesis. Read: Sections 12 and 13. Capstone: take a new locus, run the pipeline, read its A4, decide whether it is built as a switch or a rheostat, and then state — with reasons — why you cannot read its quantitative setting from the sequence, and what data you would need.

  10. Stage 9 — Mastery. Confirm the rubric below; the fastest check is to teach a newcomer Stages 1, 2, and 6 using only the analogy.

Common traps (kept visible because we hit them)

  • The lineage confound. A mouse-vs-elephant difference can look like “size” yet be lineage; always control within a family (cat vs tiger). This fooled us once with a clean-looking 5/55/5 signal (Section 11.7).

  • Hand-set coordinates →\rightarrow deformity. Placing body parts at chosen numbers malforms the body; read coordinates from the genome’s position code (Section 10.2).

  • Equal-length Hamming artifact. Comparing two same-length but unaligned sequences gives fake low identity; always align first (GDF15/IRS4 looked divergent until aligned, then conserved).

  • Looking for a “size gene.” There is not one; size is global — read the whole or the runtime, not a single locus.

  • Truncated annotations. Predicted transcript models can cut a 3′3'UTR and undercount sites; check sequence length before trusting a count.

  • Short-sequence pipeline crash. Loci shorter than the window broke the old pipeline; use the Version-7 fix (window clipped to length).

Mastery rubric

You have learned the method when you can: (1) run and reproduce the core pipeline and verify a result against DATA_LOCK/MANIFEST; (2) interpret a new region’s A4 with the five steps; (3) classify any feature as Layer-1 position or Layer-2 quantity, and a cis element as switch or rheostat; (4) design a within-family control for a claimed cross-species signal; (5) explain the size result without contradiction — in the genome, global, not localizable; and (6) recognize and avoid the six traps above.

References

  1. Foss EJ, et al. Identification of replication origins in S. cerevisiae. eLife 2024. (MCM-ChEC; GEO GSE242131.)

  2. Hennion M, et al. FORK-seq: replication landscape of the S. cerevisiae genome by nanopore sequencing. Genome Biol. 2020;21:125.

  3. Müller CA, et al. The dynamics of genome replication using deep sequencing. Nucleic Acids Res. 2014;42:e3. (Replication timing; GSM1180749.)

  4. Boemo MA. Regulation of replication timing in S. cerevisiae. PLoS Comput. Biol. 2025. (Limiting firing-factor model; repository rb2065.)

  5. Rossi MJ, et al. A high-resolution protein architecture of the budding yeast genome. Nature 2021;592:309–314. (ChIP-exo; CEGRcode/2021-Rossi_Nature.)

  6. NucleosomeDensity repository: per-base nucleosome density and the sacCer3 reference.

  7. Cross-species nucleosome repeat length (human, fission yeast, echinoderm sperm).

  8. Avsec Ž, et al. Effective gene expression prediction from sequence (Enformer). Nat. Methods 2021;18:1196–1203; and AlphaGenome (2025).

  9. Data sources used directly: NCBI genome assemblies (E. coli strains; six eukaryotes), 1000-Genomes-style chromosome-21 variant files (YRI, CEU), RefSeq NM_000546.6 (TP53 mRNA), and the anvi’o Ribosomal_RNA_18S model (merenlab/anvio).
